content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

March 7, 1977

Mr. Patrick J. Sampson
City Attorney, Pamona
Box 660
Pomona, California 91769

Dear Mr. Sampson:

You ask in your letter of February 24, 1977, whether a Vehicle
Parking District Commission formed pursuant to Section 31500 et seq.,
of the Streets and Highways Code, is a "local government agency"
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82041. A local govern-
ment agency within the meaning of this section is required to develop
a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code Section 87300.

It is clear that Vehicle Parking District Commissions are "local
government agencies" and that their members are "public officials"
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Such Districts may
levy assessments or reassessments on land within the District, issue
bonds secured by land in the District; and the District Commission may,
in fact, control parking facilities within the District. Such powers
render the District a "local government agency" within the meaning
of Section 82041 and render the District's Commission members "public
officials" within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 and
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

In addition, you ask whether a city council, which is a code
reviewing body for such a Vehicle Parking District, "...could require
disclosure of interests in real property and investments and income
of those Commissioners only within the District for which the Com-
missioner serves, and perhaps areas contiguous thereto, or must the
city council require disclosure of interests city-wide."

An agency or its code reviewing body can compel disclosure of
interests of persons covered by a Conflict of Interest Code only
when it is reasonably foreseeable that the interest may be mater-
ially financially affected by any decision made or participated
in by the official by virtue of his or her position. Furthermore,
the definitions of "income", "investment", and "real property" are
all conditioned by the jurisdiction of the agency. Thus, as a
general rule, real property investments and income of designated

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION [?]A [?]1700 [?]

page 2

officials cannot be compelled to be disclosed within a Conflict of
Interest Code unless such interests are located, doing business, or
foreseeably may do business, in the jurisdiction of the agency.
Quite clearly, however, the Parking District is designed to serve
the needs of the entire community, notwithstanding the fact that the
authority does, in fact, represent a more narrow constituency.
In such circumstances, it appears imminently reasonable to require
parking commissioners to disclose interests in areas contiguous to
the District, though perhaps not city-wide. The ultimate question
is the scope of the authority of the District Commission and its
ability to materially affect interests outside the District but
within the city.

Under the facts as presented, we cannot answer the question
with greater specificity. If you desire further advice with res-
pect to this matter, please do not hesitate to call on us.

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflict of Interest Division
(916) 322-6444

DLS:mfn

sections.question: Whether a Vehicle Parking District Commission formed pursuant to Section 31500 et seq., of the Streets and Highways Code, is a "local government agency" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82041, and whether a city council, as a code reviewing body for such a Vehicle Parking District, could require disclosure of interests in real property and investments and income of these Commissioners only within the District for which the Commissioner serves, and perhaps areas contiguous thereto, or must the city council require disclosure of interests city-wide.
sections.conclusion: It is clear that Vehicle Parking District Commissions are "local government agencies" and that their members are "public officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. An agency or its code reviewing body can compel disclosure of interests only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the interest may be materially financially affected by any decision made by the official. Real property investments and income cannot be compelled to be disclosed unless such interests are located, doing business, or foreseeably may do business, in the jurisdiction of the agency. Under the facts presented, it appears imminently reasonable to require parking commissioners to disclose interests in areas contiguous to the District, though perhaps not city-wide, as the District is designed to serve the needs of the entire community. The ultimate question is the scope of the authority of the District Commission and its ability to materially affect interests outside the District but within the city.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a Vehicle Parking District Commission qualify as a "local government agency" under the Political Reform Act, and must it require commissioners to disclose financial interests city-wide or only within the district and contiguous areas?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Vehicle Parking District Commissions are local government agencies whose members must disclose financial interests. Disclosure scope should be limited to the district and contiguous areas based on the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the commission's decisions, rather than requiring city-wide disclosure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has implicit Q/A structure without formal section headers. Question reconstructed from letter content and FPPC response. Clear conclusion and reasoning provided by Chief of Conflict of Interest Division.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a Vehicle Parking District Commission formed pursuant to Section 31500 et seq., of the Streets and Highways Code, is a "local government agency" within the meaning of Government Code Section 82041, and whether a city council, as a code reviewing body for such a Vehicle Parking District, could require disclosure of interests in real property and investments and income of these Commissioners only within the District for which the Commissioner serves, and perhaps areas contiguous thereto, or must the city council require disclosure of interests city-wide.

CONCLUSION: It is clear that Vehicle Parking District Commissions are "local government agencies" and that their members are "public officials" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. An agency or its code reviewing body can compel disclosure of interests only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the interest may be materially financially affected by any decision made by the official. Real property investments and income cannot be compelled to be disclosed unless such interests are located, doing business, or foreseeably may do business, in the jurisdiction of the agency. Under the facts presented, it appears imminently reasonable to require parking commissioners to disclose interests in areas contiguous to the District, though perhaps not city-wide, as the District is designed to serve the needs of the entire community. The ultimate question is the scope of the authority of the District Commission and its ability to materially affect interests outside the District but within the city.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Vehicle Parking District Commissions constitute local government agencies requiring conflict of interest codes, and that commissioners should disclose financial interests in the district and contiguous areas based on the foreseeable scope of the commission's authority.
